definition: A linked list is a data structure made of nodes where each node stores data and a reference to the next node in the sequence.
deepExplanation: A singly linked list is not stored as one continuous block like an array. The list is found by starting at the head reference and following each node's next reference until null. This makes insertion at the front simple because the new node only needs to point to the old head. It also means random access is slow because the only way to reach the kth node is to walk through the chain one node at a time.
whyItMatters: Linked lists teach reference-based thinking, which is essential for stacks, queues, trees, graphs, and many pointer manipulation problems.
analogy: Think of a treasure hunt where each clue tells you where the next clue is. You cannot jump straight to clue seven unless you follow the chain from the beginning.

codeExamples.title: Node class, traversal, and insert at front

walkthrough.text: Each Node stores a data value and a next reference to another Node.
insertFront creates a new node for the value being inserted.
The new node points to the old head, preserving the rest of the list.
The new node becomes the new head, so callers must keep this returned reference.
Traversal starts at head and repeatedly follows next until it reaches null.

codeExamples.title: Delete after a known node safely

walkthrough.text: The guard prevents deleting after a missing node or after the tail.
Store the node being removed before changing links so the operation is easy to reason about.
Bypass the removed node by pointing the current node to the removed node's next.
Clearing removed.next helps show that the removed node is no longer part of the chain.

diagrams.title: Singly Linked List Memory Model
diagrams.caption: The head reference points to the first node, and each node points to the next node until null.

diagrams.title: Insert at Front
diagrams.caption: Insertion at the front preserves the old chain by linking the new node to the old head before returning it.

commonMistakes.mistake: Overwriting next before saving the rest of the chain.
commonMistakes.whyItHappens: Students update references in the wrong order and lose access to later nodes.
commonMistakes.correction: Store important references first, then rewire links deliberately.

commonMistakes.mistake: Forgetting to stop traversal at null.
commonMistakes.whyItHappens: It is easy to write current.next without checking whether current exists.
commonMistakes.correction: Use while (current != null) for traversal and guard operations near the tail.

commonMistakes.mistake: Assuming linked lists have O(1) access by index.
commonMistakes.whyItHappens: The list looks ordered, so beginners expect array-like indexing.
commonMistakes.correction: Remember that reaching an index requires following next references from head.

bestPractices: Draw the before and after references before coding insertion or deletion.
Always keep track of the head reference because losing it loses the list.
Use guard checks for null before reading node.next.
Return the new head from operations that might change the first node.

miniQuiz.question: When inserting at the front, what should newNode.next point to before returning?
miniQuiz.explanation: Pointing newNode.next to the old head preserves the existing chain behind the new first node.

miniQuiz.question: Why is access by index O(n) in a singly linked list?
options: There is no direct jump to an index
miniQuiz.answer: There is no direct jump to an index
miniQuiz.explanation: To reach index k, the program must follow next references from the head through earlier nodes.

flashcards.front: What does the head reference store?
flashcards.back: The head reference points to the first node in the linked list.

flashcards.front: What does a node contain in a singly linked list?
flashcards.back: A data value and a next reference to the following node.
It only creates one node, points it to the old head, and returns it as the new head.
